title: Work & School
shortSummary: Talk about your job, your studies, and your workplace or classroom in Hindi.
culturalNote: Education is held in very high regard across India, and parents often make great sacrifices for their children's schooling. Many schools and offices operate in both Hindi and English, and switching between them is routine. Teachers are deeply respected; students often greet a teacher with namaste guruji and the festival of Teachers' Day (Shikshak Divas) honours them each September. In the workplace, hierarchy matters, and a senior is often addressed as sir or madam or with ji after their name. Punctuality expectations vary by setting; formal offices value it, while social timing can be more relaxed, sometimes affectionately called 'Indian Stretchable Time'. Asking someone 'aap kya kaam karte hain?' (what work do you do?) is a normal early-conversation question.

keyPhrases.native: मैं ऑफिस में काम करता हूँ
keyPhrases.transliteration: main office mein kaam karta hoon
keyPhrases.pronunciation: main OFF-iss mein kaam KUR-taa hoon

keyPhrases.native: मैं नौ बजे काम शुरू करता हूँ
keyPhrases.transliteration: main nau baje kaam shuru karta hoon
keyPhrases.pronunciation: main now BUH-jay kaam shoo-ROO KUR-taa hoon

keyPhrases.native: मैं घर से काम करता हूँ
keyPhrases.transliteration: main ghar se kaam karta hoon
keyPhrases.pronunciation: main ghar say kaam KUR-taa hoon

grammar.explanation: To say you like something, Hindi uses pasand hai (literally 'is liked') with the liker marked by ko or the pronoun mujhe. Mujhe ganit pasand hai means 'maths is pleasing to me', so the thing liked is the grammatical subject.

examples.native: मुझे हिंदी पसंद है। (mujhe Hindi pasand hai.)
उसे संगीत पसंद है। (use sangeet pasand hai.)

grammar.commonMistake: Saying main pasand karta hoon for simple 'I like'; the natural phrase is mujhe ... pasand hai.

grammar.explanation: Many work and study activities are formed by a noun plus karna (to do): kaam karna (to work), shuru karna (to start), padhaai karna (to study). The karna part conjugates while the noun stays fixed.

examples.native: मैं काम करता हूँ। (main kaam karta hoon.)
वह पढ़ाई करती है। (vah padhaai karti hai.)

grammar.commonMistake: Conjugating the noun instead of karna; only karna changes for person and gender.

dialogue.setup: Lisa, learning Hindi, chats with Deepak about their work and study.

lines.speaker: Lisa
lines.native: दीपक, आप क्या काम करते हैं?
lines.transliteration: Deepak, aap kya kaam karte hain?
lines.english: Deepak, what work do you do?

lines.speaker: Deepak
lines.native: मैं एक ऑफिस में काम करता हूँ। और आप?
lines.transliteration: main ek office mein kaam karta hoon. aur aap?
lines.english: I work in an office. And you?

lines.speaker: Lisa
lines.native: मैं एक छात्रा हूँ। मैं हिंदी पढ़ती हूँ।
lines.transliteration: main ek chhaatra hoon. main Hindi padhti hoon.
lines.english: I'm a student. I study Hindi.

lines.speaker: Deepak
lines.native: बहुत अच्छा! क्या आपको हिंदी पसंद है?
lines.transliteration: bahut achha! kya aapko Hindi pasand hai?
lines.english: Very nice! Do you like Hindi?

lines.speaker: Lisa
lines.native: जी हाँ, मुझे हिंदी बहुत पसंद है।
lines.transliteration: ji haan, mujhe Hindi bahut pasand hai.
lines.english: Yes, I like Hindi very much.

lines.speaker: Deepak
lines.native: आप कितने बजे पढ़ाई शुरू करती हैं?
lines.transliteration: aap kitne baje padhaai shuru karti hain?
lines.english: What time do you start studying?

lines.speaker: Lisa
lines.native: मैं सुबह नौ बजे शुरू करती हूँ।
lines.transliteration: main subah nau baje shuru karti hoon.
lines.english: I start at nine in the morning.

quiz.question: How do you say 'I like Hindi' in Hindi?
quiz.correctAnswer: मुझे हिंदी पसंद है (mujhe Hindi pasand hai)
quiz.explanation: Pasand hai means 'is liked', with the liker marked by mujhe.

quiz.question: Which phrase means 'I work in an office' (man speaking)?
options: मैं ऑफिस में काम करता हूँ
quiz.correctAnswer: मैं ऑफिस में काम करता हूँ
quiz.explanation: Office mein kaam karta hoon means 'I work in an office'.

quiz.question: वह पढ़ाई _____ है। (She studies)
quiz.explanation: In the compound verb padhaai karna, karna conjugates to feminine karti.

quiz.question: What does 'आज मेरी परीक्षा है' mean?
Arrange: करता / मैं / हूँ / काम (I work)
quiz.correctAnswer: मैं काम करता हूँ (main kaam karta hoon)
quiz.explanation: Subject, the noun kaam, then the verb karta hoon at the end.
